class Solution {
public:
    int compareVersion(string version1, string version2) {
        // Split into parts
        auto split = [](const string& version) {
            vector<string> parts;
            istringstream iss(version);
            string part;
            while (getline(iss, part, '.')) {
                parts.push_back(part);
            }
            return parts;
        };
        vector<string> parts1 = split(version1);
        vector<string> parts2 = split(version2);
        int max_len = max(parts1.size(), parts2.size());
        for (int i = 0; i < max_len; ++i) {
            int num1 = 0, num2 = 0;
            if (i < parts1.size()) {
                num1 = stoi(parts1[i]);
            }
            if (i < parts2.size()) {
                num2 = stoi(parts2[i]);
            }
            if (num1 != num2) {
                return (num1 < num2) ? -1 : 1;
            }
        }
        return 0;
    }
};

Now, time and space complexity.

Time complexity: O(n + m), where n and m are the lengths of version1 and version2. Because splitting takes O(n) and O(m) time. Then, the loop runs for max(k, l) times, where k and l are the number of parts. Each stoi is O(1) since the parts are up to 500 characters? Wait, no. The stoi function for a string of digits takes O(p) time where p is the length of the string. However, since each version string is up to 500 characters, the total time for all stoi operations is O(n + m) overall. Because the sum of the lengths of all parts is O(n) for version1, same for version2. So the total time is O(n + m). So overall, time complexity is O(n + m).

Space complexity: O(k + l), where k and l are the number of parts. Since each part is stored in vectors. The number of parts can be up to O(min(n, 500)), but since each part is at least 1 character and separated by a dot, the maximum number of parts is min(n, 500). But for space, it's O(k + l), but since n is up to 500, this is acceptable. So space is O(k + l) but in practice, O(n + m).

Energy implications: LOW, since the code is efficient and uses standard library functions.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    int compareVersion(string version1, string version2) {
        auto split = [](const string& version) {
            vector<string> parts;
            istringstream iss(version);
            string part;
            while (getline(iss, part, '.')) {
                parts.push_back(part);
            }
            return parts;
        };
        vector<string> parts1 = split(version1);
        vector<string> parts2 = split(version2);
        int max_len = max(parts1.size(), parts2.size());
        for (int i = 0; i < max_len; ++i) {
            int num1 = 0, num2 = 0;
            if (i < parts1.size()) {
                num1 = stoi(parts1[i]);
            }
            if (i < parts2.size()) {
                num2 = stoi(parts2[i]);
            }
            if (num1 != num2) {
                return (num1 < num2) ? -1 : 1;
            }
        }
        return 0;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n + m) where n and m are the lengths of version1 and version2
space complexity: O(k + l) where k and l are the number of parts in version1 and version2
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution splits both version strings into integer parts, then compares each part sequentially. It handles missing parts by treating them as 0. Key strengths include simplicity and linear processing. Weaknesses involve potential overhead from string-to-integer conversions and storing split parts.
